mnemonics.title: OS SERVICES MEMORY AID
mnemonics.content: REMEMBER: OS PROVIDES CORE SERVICES USING MEMORY, FILES, AND DEVICES FOR SECURITY. Mnemonic: P-M-F-D-S-N (Process, Memory, File, Device, Security, Networking)

mnemonics.title: BOOT SEQUENCE
mnemonics.content: POWER ON -> POST -> BOOT LOADER -> KERNEL -> INIT -> RUNLEVEL. Mnemonic: P-B-K-I-R (Please Bring Kernel Initialy Run)

mnemonics.title: DUAL MODE IMPORTANCE
mnemonics.content: PROTECTS SYSTEM FROM USER MISTAKES AND MALICIOUS CODE. Mnemonic: P-R-I (Privilege Restriction Isolates)

mnemonics.title: KERNEL TYPES COMPARISON
mnemonics.content: MONO: ALL IN ONE (FAST, LESS SECURE) vs MICRO: MINIMAL KERNEL (SLOW, MORE SECURE). Mnemonic: M-F-S vs Mi-S-S (Mono Fast/Secure vs Micro Slow/Secure)

acronyms.usage: Set of function definitions for building software applications
Firmware that initializes hardware during boot process
Modern replacement for BIOS with better security and faster boot
Mechanisms for processes to communicate and synchronize
Code that handles hardware interrupt requests
Unix-like standard that defines OS compatibility
Text-based interface for interacting with the OS

visualTips.visualization: Think of a bank: User Mode is the customer area (limited access), Kernel Mode is the vault (full access). You need authorization (system call) to move between areas.
Draw a circle representing kernel. In monolithic, everything is inside the circle. In microkernel, only essential services are inside, others float outside connected by lines.
Draw a stair-like diagram: User Program (bottom) -> API Library -> System Call Interface -> Kernel (top). Each step represents privilege level increase.
Draw a horizontal timeline: Power -> BIOS/POST -> Boot Loader (reads MBR) -> Kernel Load -> Kernel Decompress -> Init (systemd) -> Login. Each arrow represents a transition.

analogies.analogy: Just as a government provides essential services (defense, courts, infrastructure) to citizens, the OS provides services (memory, files, security) to applications. Citizens (apps) request services through proper channels (system calls), not directly.

The kernel is like the heart of the OS - it runs continuously in kernel mode, managing all vital operations. Just as the heart controls blood flow, the kernel controls resource flow between hardware and software.

Just as bank customers cannot directly access the vault, user programs cannot directly access hardware. They must go through the teller (system call interface) who has proper authorization (kernel mode).
Device drivers are like translators between two people who speak different languages. The OS speaks in generic commands, hardware speaks in specific signals. Drivers translate between them.

Imagine the CPU as someone in a meeting. An interrupt is like an urgent phone call - they must pause the meeting, answer the call (handle interrupt), then resume the meeting (return to previous task).
